title: The Last Dronie
creator: chrisxgxc (Chris Castor)
award_or_showcase_provenance: Winner, Dronie category, AirVuz's 2nd Annual Drone Video Awards (winners announced 2019-02-18); also featured as an AirVuz Staff Pick / social-media feature (January 2018).
notes: MANUALLY CURATED WORKED EXAMPLE -- one exemplar only, per spec AC3.4/Scope-out (no automated scraping/bulk downloading was performed to produce this record). Verified this session via WebFetch of the AirVuz page itself (https://www.airvuz.com/video/The-Last-Dronie?id=5a669826f65b5f4bd48704bd), which returned the exact title ('The Last Dronie'), creator/uploader ('chrisxgxc', real name Chris Castor credited as Director/Pilot/VFX), a plot description (a zombie-apocalypse scenario in which survivors use a drone to map an escape route from a horde of undead), and explicit award text: 'Winner in the Dronie category for the 2nd Annual Drone Video Awards' plus 'AirVuz Staff Pick' / social-media feature (January 2018). A follow-up WebSearch ('"The Last Dronie" chrisxgxc AirVuz Drone Video Awards Dronie category') independently corroborated this, returning both the AirVuz video page itself and the AirVuz Drone Video Awards landing page, and additionally surfaced that 2nd Annual Drone Video Awards winners were announced 2019-02-18 and that the piece was shot entirely on a DJI Phantom 4 with VFX/editing done in Adobe After Effects, Cinema 4D, and other 3D applications. A direct WebFetch of https://www.airvuz.com/drone-video-awards (attempted to independently cross-check the winner list/category description) returned HTTP 404 -- that page appears to have been retired or restructured since the award was announced -- so the award claim rests on the AirVuz video page's own text plus the WebSearch corroboration, not a second independently-fetched primary source; noting this as a modest gap in the provenance chain rather than concealing it. `platform` is 'other' per this session's own verification (airvuz.com is neither youtube.com nor vimeo.com), matching the discovery-stage flag. `is_instagram` does not apply here (source is airvuz.com, not an Instagram URL), so the Instagram media-extraction prohibition is not triggered, but `local_media_path` is still left null unconditionally per this project's blanket no-download policy. `license_category` is set to 'all-rights-reserved' based on DIRECT verification, not merely the conservative default: this session's WebFetch of the AirVuz page explicitly returned 'Copyright (c)2026 AirVuz. All rights reserved. (No Creative Commons license mentioned)' -- a confirmed, not inferred, all-rights-reserved status. Consequently no footage was or will be downloaded, and `flagged_for_user_signoff` is false (this task's flagging rule only triggers when a cc-by/cc0/public-domain/owned license is independently verified, which is not the case here). `duration` is left null: the AirVuz page did not surface a runtime/duration value in this session's WebFetch, and rather than fabricate a plausible-looking number, this field stays unset pending manual playback confirmation. `scores` (sharpness/exposure/motion_smoothness) are `scores_provenance: manually_estimated` placeholder judgments, NOT measurements from Capability 1's actual scoring functions (scoring_sharpness.py/scoring_exposure.py/scoring_motion_smoothness.py) -- no source file was downloaded to run those against, per the no-bulk-download constraint. The estimates reflect this being a professionally produced, award-recognized short film with a dedicated director/VFX/sound-design/cast credit list, but shot on a 2016-era DJI Phantom 4 (a mid-tier 1/2.3in-sensor camera by current standards) and built around VFX-heavy narrative compositing rather than pure landscape/aerial cinematography -- so scores were set moderately (sharpness=0.72, exposure=0.73, motion_smoothness=0.75), somewhat below this pack's top-tier broadcaster/cinematic exemplars (e.g. bbc-earth-aerial.json's 0.78/0.75/0.80) but broadly consistent with other verified-award short-form exemplars in this pack. `composite_score` IS a real computation: produced by calling this repo's own `drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.composite.compute_composite_score` with these three manually-estimated inputs (sharpness=0.72, exposure=0.73, motion_smoothness=0.75, composition=None) and `drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.weights.default_weights(weights_version='default-v1')` (equal 1/3 weighting on sharpness/exposure/motion_smoothness, composition weight fixed at 0.0), returning 0.7333333333333333 (rounded to 0.7333 here) via `.venv/bin/python -c "..."` in this session -- so the composite arithmetic itself is genuine and reproducible, even though its inputs are estimates rather than measurements. `editorial_style.format` is set to 'short-form-reel': the AirVuz page itself frames this as a short competition/showcase entry judged specifically in the 'Dronie' category -- a recognized short-form drone-selfie subgenre -- consistent with the task's own flagging rationale. No live playback was performed in this session (browser-automation tools were not invoked), so `review_method` is 'text_provenance_only' and `estimated_cut_count`/`avg_shot_length_seconds`/`transition_styles_observed` are left null/empty rather than guessed from the plot description alone.

editorial_style.pacing_notes: Not independently reviewed via playback in this session; the page/description indicates a narrative VFX short (drone-selfie premise: survivors use a drone to scout an escape route from a zombie horde) rather than a pure long-take aerial cinematography piece, implying likely faster, narrative-driven cutting consistent with the 'short-form-reel'/Dronie-category classification -- but exact cut count and shot lengths are unverified and left null rather than guessed.
